topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope sat on my desk, stark white against the dark wood. Inside, the letter was short, formal, and utterly final: Sarah was moving to Seattle. I stared at the words, the ink blurring slightly under my gaze. It wasn't a surprise, not really; we’d talked about bigger things, but this felt like a physical blow. My hands started to tremble, the cheap paper crinkling loudly under my grip. I traced the edge of the envelope with a fingertip, feeling a sudden, sharp tightness in my chest, as if something vital had just been squeezed out of me. I wanted to shout, to demand an explanation, but my throat felt constricted, dry and useless. I just sat there, the silence of my apartment amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears, a hollow echo where easy laughter used to reside.

[story 2]

I watched Mark pack his worn duffel bag, the way he folded his favorite hoodie with unnecessary care. Every item seemed to hold a memory, a tangible piece of the life we’d built together in this city. When he finally looked up, his eyes were bright, almost too bright, and the genuine happiness in them struck me like a physical jolt. "Seattle," he said, a smile stretching across his face. The sound of his voice, light and forward-looking, felt like sandpaper against my nerves. I swallowed hard, my mouth suddenly desert-dry. I found myself staring at the floor, focusing intensely on a scuff mark on the linoleum, wishing the ground would simply swallow me whole. A strange, heavy weight settled in my stomach, a dull ache that made it hard to breathe deeply. I managed a weak, "That's... big," but the sound was thin, reedy.

[story 3]

The coffee went cold before I even reached for it. I kept re-reading the itinerary Sarah had emailed—the boxes checked, the dates confirmed. It was all so neat, so organized, and the reality of her leaving felt messy and chaotic, spilling over into every corner of my vision. I stood by the window, the city lights blurring into streaks of indifferent color. My shoulders slumped, a profound sense of deflation washing over me. I felt a desperate urge to rewind the last few months, to find a moment where I could have somehow stopped this trajectory. A hot prickle of unshed tears burned behind my eyes, and I quickly blinked them away, scrubbing at the sudden dampness with the back of my hand. It was a futile gesture, just a small, pathetic attempt to smooth over the sudden, gaping void opening up inside my chest.
